d17s0.what: Kingman is where you leave the interstate for the longest surviving uninterrupted stretch of Route 66 — 255 km of the original road to Seligman that I-40 never swallowed. The museum in the 1907 powerhouse tells the road's whole arc: Dust Bowl exodus, neon boom, bypass and rebirth.
see: The Route 66 Museum's Dust Bowl room — Okie jalopies packed like Grapes of Wrath, because this literally was that road
The electric-vehicle museum in the same building, weirdly delightful
tips: 30 min max — the road ahead is the real exhibit
Top up gas here; the historic stretch is beautiful and empty
The climb east crosses Sitgreaves Pass alternative? No — you're on the flat eastern stretch; enjoy 100 km of open two-lane with Burma-Shave signs
d17s0.fact: Route 66 (1926–1985) was decommissioned entirely — it doesn't officially exist. Arizona's stretch survived because Seligman's town barber refused to let it die (you're about to meet his shop).

d17s1.what: Juan Delgadillo built the Snow Cap in 1953 from scrap lumber off the Santa Fe railroad and spent 50 years serving 'cheeseburgers with cheese' and squirting customers with a trick mustard bottle (it's string). His brother Angel — the town barber — founded the association that got Route 66 its 'Historic' designation in 1987 and saved every town on this road.
see: The menu gags, the door handle that isn't, the mustard trick — order a milkshake and a 'dead chicken' and play along
The backyard of vintage cars and 70 years of accumulated Americana
Angel's barbershop/visitor center two doors down — his family still runs it; the guestbook has signatures from every country on Earth
tips: Cash-friendly, line moves fast, eat at the picnic tables
Seligman was Pixar's model for Radiator Springs — John Lasseter interviewed Angel here; the whole town leans into it and has earned the right
d17s1.fact: When I-40 bypassed Seligman in 1978, businesses died in a single day. Angel Delgadillo's response — 'they took our road, we'll take it back' — is why 'Historic Route 66' signs exist anywhere in America.

d17s2.what: Williams was the LAST town on all 3,940 km of Route 66 to be bypassed — it held out until October 13, 1984, with lawyers, and only surrendered when the state promised three exits. Tonight it's the best-preserved slice of the neon road anywhere: two one-way streets of diners, saloons and motel signs buzzing pink against the pines.
see: The neon loop after dark: walk both one-way streets (Route 66 eastbound, Railroad Ave westbound) — 15 unhurried minutes
The Grand Canyon Railway depot (1908) — the vintage train still departs for the rim every morning with staged train robberies on the way back
Pete's Route 66 Gas Station Museum if it's open — one man's perfect little collection
tips: Dinner: Station 66 (pizza in the old gas station, patio) or Goldies Route 66 Diner for the full chrome-and-vinyl experience
Fill the tank tonight — tomorrow is all park roads
Elk wander into town at dusk; that's normal here
d17s2.fact: On bypass day in 1984, Bobby Troup — who wrote '(Get Your Kicks on) Route 66' — came and sang it on a flatbed truck as the last stoplight on the mother road went dark.

d18s0.what: The Grand Canyon does not photograph, describe, or prepare-for: 446 km long, up to 29 km wide, 1.8 km deep, and your first look at it from Mather Point is a genuine neurological event. The rocks at the river are 1.8 BILLION years old — the canyon exposes 40% of Earth's history in one wall.
see: The first 10 seconds — put the phone down for them, then take the photos

Read the wall like a book: the top layer (Kaibab limestone, 270 M yrs) was a sea floor; the great red cliffs (Redwall, 340 M) another sea; the dark basement rock (Vishnu schist) is older than complex life
California condors — 3-metre wingspan, numbered wing tags, one of the rarest birds on Earth; they patrol the rim thermals most mornings
tips: Sunrise (~5:20am, no DST here) is the hardcore option and worth it once in a life; otherwise be parked in the Village by 8:30am before lots fill
Walk west on the flat, paved Rim Trail from Mather toward Yavapai Point (30 min) — the crowds evaporate 400 m from any parking lot
Yavapai Geology Museum (free, on your walk) has the canyon's best explainer and a huge window — 20 min well spent
The rim is at 2,100 m: sunscreen works overtime and beer works double, plan accordingly
d18s0.fact: The Colorado River did all of this in about 5–6 million years — a geologic instant. The canyon deepens the thickness of a sheet of paper each year, and the river you'll glimpse is 1.6 km down yet you'll hear it in the quiet spots.

d18s1.what: Bright Angel is THE trail — the mule route, the fault-line path Havasupai people used for centuries — and in July it's also where most rescues happen. The iron rule: going down is optional, coming up is mandatory, and July heat rises 5–8°C for every 300 m you descend.
see: The tunnel through the rock 200 m down, and the switchbacks stacked below like ribbon
Mule trains (they have right of way — stand quietly on the inside of the trail)
1.5-Mile Resthouse: your absolute turnaround — shade, water (usually), and a very different canyon than the rim showed you
tips: Start before 8am or don't start; be climbing out before the heat, not in it
2× the water you think, salty snacks, hat; the climb out takes twice the descent time
Even 20 minutes down changes the canyon from a picture into a place — do that much at minimum
NEVER attempt rim-to-river-and-back in a day; the warning signs quote the marathon runner who died trying
d18s1.fact: The resthouses were built in the 1930s by the CCC; the trail's water pipeline is so old and burst-prone the park is replacing it — check the tap status board at the trailhead like everyone else.

d18s2.what: Hopi Point juts further into the canyon than anywhere else on Hermit Road — sunset from here paints 150 km of buttes in sequence as the shadow line sweeps east, and the river shows itself in two silver bends. This is the grand finale of the western half of your trip; treat it ceremonially.
see: The named 'temples' igniting one by one: Isis, Osiris, Shiva — 1880s geologist Clarence Dutton named them for gods because ordinary words had already failed
Two Colorado River reaches visible below (rare on the South Rim)
The AFTER-show: 15–20 min past sunset the sky goes violet and the canyon exhales; most people leave exactly too early
tips: Hermit Road is shuttle-only: red line from the Village, every 10–15 min; board 60–75 min before sunset to get position
The point is popular — walk 100 m along the rim trail either side for elbow room
Headlamp/phone light for the shuttle stop afterwards, and the fleece: rim temperature drops fast at 2,100 m
d18s2.fact: Dutton's 1882 survey report on this canyon is considered literature — he invented a whole descriptive vocabulary ('an innovation in nature') because European landscape language simply had no words for what you're looking at.

d19s0.what: Mary Colter's 1932 Desert View Watchtower is a 21-metre 'ruin' that never was — she studied ancestral Puebloan towers for months, then built a steel-framed homage so convincing people assume it's ancient. It's the canyon's easternmost overlook and your literal parting view.
see: Inside: Hopi artist Fred Kabotie's murals spiral up the stairwell — the Snake Legend room is the masterpiece; climb to the top gallery windows
The river's big bend below — the canyon opens here and you can finally see the Colorado properly, green and working
The Painted Desert beginning to smear pink across the eastern horizon — that's where you're headed
tips: 45 min including the tower climb; restrooms + trading post here
This exit (east, Hwy 64 to Cameron) is slower but immeasurably better than backtracking — the Little Colorado gorge overlooks en route are free and startling
Navajo vendors at Cameron and the gorge overlooks sell real jewelry at fair prices if you want the trip's souvenir
d19s0.fact: Colter, one of very few female architects of her era, designed most of the canyon's great buildings. For the tower she had workers study each stone; she once made them tear down a wall because the 'ruin' looked too neat.

d19s1.what: Fifty thousand years ago an iron-nickel asteroid the size of a house hit Arizona at ~45,000 km/h and vaporized, leaving a bowl 1.2 km wide and 170 m deep that looks like it happened last Tuesday — the driest air on the continent preserved Earth's best impact crater almost perfectly.
see: The rim view (you cannot see the crater until the last 10 steps — the reveal is theatrical)
The 300 kg Holsinger fragment in the museum — the largest surviving piece; you can touch 4.5-billion-year-old space metal
Apollo astronaut training photos: NASA sent every moonwalker here to learn crater geology
tips: ~$29/person, privately owned (the Barringer family has held it since 1903), 60–75 min with the rim tour
It is ferociously windy on the rim — hats become kites
Skip-decision guide: if you've never seen an impact crater, go; if budget/time is tight, the canyon this morning outranks it
d19s1.fact: Daniel Barringer spent 26 years and his fortune drilling for the iron mass — which had vaporized on impact. His bankruptcy proved the impact theory that made the site famous: the meteor's absence is the evidence.

d19s2.what: 'Well, I'm a-standin' on a corner in Winslow, Arizona…' — one line in the Eagles' first single (1972, written by Jackson Browne with Glenn Frey) has kept this bypassed railroad town alive for 50 years. The town built the corner to match the song: flatbed Ford, girl reflection and all.
see: The bronze troubadour statue (it's Browne-ish; a Frey statue joined it in 2016), the painted flatbed Ford, the trompe-l'oeil mural

La Posada, two blocks away: Mary Colter's 1930 masterpiece hotel, rescued from demolition — walk the gardens and lobby free; the Turquoise Room does a genuinely great lunch if you're ahead of schedule
tips: 20 minutes, free, ineluctably cheerful — someone will offer to take your photo; accept
The gift shop plays the song on loop; the staff have heard it ~40,000 times and are still nice
d19s2.fact: Browne wrote the verse stranded here after his car broke down — the 'girl in a flatbed Ford' was real, slowing down to look at his friend. The town's economy now measurably runs on those 17 words.

d19s3.what: A forest that became gemstone: 225 million years ago this was a Triassic river delta of 60-m conifers; buried in volcanic ash, their wood was replaced cell-by-cell with quartz — logs of solid agate and amethyst now weathering out of pastel hills. It's also the only national park containing a stretch of Route 66.
see: Crystal Forest (the essential 20-min loop): logs shattered into cordwood rounds of red, purple and gold crystal — they broke like glass when the ground eroded from under them
The 1932 Studebaker at the Route 66 pullout — rusted where the mother road crossed the park
Painted Desert overlooks at the north end as you exit toward I-40 — the badlands go full watercolor near sunset
Newspaper Rock: 650+ petroglyphs viewable by spotting scope
tips: The park road is a 45-km drive-through connecting I-40 to US-180 — enter south (Rainbow Forest) or north; with your route, north entrance in/out or the full transit both work; budget ~1.5 h
Taking even a pebble of petrified wood is a federal offense — buy legal pieces (collected off-park) at the shops outside for a few dollars
Gates close in the evening (~7-8pm summer) — this is why today's leave-by matters
d19s3.fact: The visitor center displays 'conscience letters' — decades of returned stolen wood fragments mailed back with apologies describing ruined marriages, lost jobs and general misfortune attributed to the 'curse.' The park neither confirms nor denies.

d19s4.what: You arrive tonight in the oldest capital city in the United States — founded 1610, a decade before the Mayflower — 2,100 m up at the foot of the Sangre de Cristo mountains, where every building by law wears adobe brown and the food has been fusion for 400 years.
see: Tonight: just dinner and the walk to the Plaza if energy allows — it glows under the portal lamps
Full exploration is tomorrow morning (it's on the itinerary)
tips: Dinner: Tomasita's (locals' red chile, by the railyard) or The Shed (half a block off the Plaza, book or queue) — when asked 'red or green?' the correct answer is 'Christmas' (both)
You gained an hour crossing into Mountain time — spend it on dinner
2,100 m again: hydrate, the margaritas here are famously load-bearing
d19s4.fact: Santa Fe's full name is La Villa Real de la Santa Fe de San Francisco de Asís — the Royal Town of the Holy Faith of St. Francis of Assisi. Locals have sensibly shortened it for 400 years.

d20s0.what: The Plaza has been Santa Fe's living room since 1610: the Palace of the Governors along its north side is the oldest continuously occupied public building in the US — Spanish, Mexican, Confederate (for two weeks) and American flags have all flown over it. Under its portal, Native artisans have sold jewelry by permit since the 1930s.
see: The Palace portal market: buy directly from the artist — every piece is juried authentic (turquoise + silver here is the real thing, unlike much of Route 66)
San Miguel Chapel (5 min south): oldest church structure in the US, adobe from ~1610
Loretto Chapel's 'miraculous' helix staircase — two full 360° turns, no central support, built 1878 by a mysterious carpenter who vanished unpaid
Cathedral Basilica of St. Francis — the French Romanesque surprise Bishop Lamy built (of Willa Cather's 'Death Comes for the Archbishop')
tips: Morning is coffee + strolling pace: 2–3 hours does the Plaza core
Green-chile breakfast burrito at Tia Sophia's (they claim to have invented it) two blocks west
Parking garages on Water St and San Francisco St beat circling the Plaza
d20s0.fact: The Santa Fe Trail from Missouri ended literally at this square — wagon ruts are still visible east of town. You'll be driving its replacement (I-25/US-285 corridor) out this afternoon, in reverse, like every trader since 1821.

d20s1.what: Ten Cadillacs (1949–1963, tracing the rise and fall of the tailfin) buried nose-down in a Texas wheat field at the same angle as the Great Pyramid's sides — a 1974 prank by San Francisco art collective Ant Farm, bankrolled by eccentric Amarillo millionaire Stanley Marsh 3. Spray-painting them isn't vandalism; it's the exhibit.
see: Your own layer: the cars wear thousands of coats — paint has measurably thickened them by centimetres; whatever you paint will be gone under someone else's by tomorrow, which is the artwork's actual point
Tailfin evolution, still legible under the paint: the '49 has none, the '59 has the biggest fins ever made
tips: Any hardware store or the on-site vending sells cans (~$5–8); bring one per person + one to gift a family that forgot
Sunset light (~8:45pm CDT) sets the field glowing — golden hour here is the photo
It's a 300-m walk into the field from the frontage road gate; take your empties out
Wind advisory: paint downwind of your own shirt
d20s1.fact: Marsh's instruction to Ant Farm was that the art should 'baffle the locals.' Instead the locals adopted it so completely that when the cars were once repainted a uniform color for a documentary, Amarillans had them re-graffitied within 24 hours.

d20s2.what: The Big Texan (1960) is the Route 66 steakhouse that moved to I-40 when the road died and got bigger: home of the 72 oz steak challenge — eat a 2 kg top sirloin plus shrimp cocktail, baked potato, salad and roll in 60 minutes and it's free. About 1 in 6 finish. You're here to eat a reasonable steak and watch someone attempt an unreasonable one.
see: Challengers eat on a raised stage under a countdown clock — the house announces attempts; cheer accordingly
The Hall of Fame photos: record holder Molly Schuyler ate THREE in 20 minutes total, a fact that will haunt you
Peak Texas décor: longhorn everything, shooting gallery, in-house brewery (the beers are legitimately good)
tips: Free limo with steer horns shuttles from area hotels — call them; it's the correct way to arrive
No reservation needed for normal tables; the wait moves fast
The 8–12 oz cuts are good honest steak; the challenge steak is, by all survivor accounts, not the kitchen's best work
d20s2.fact: The challenge began when founder Bob Lee watched a cowboy demolish a pound of steak and keep ordering; the one-hour, free-if-finished format is unchanged since 1962, and attempts run nightly.

d21s0.what: The U-Drop Inn (1936) is Art Deco gone wonderfully feral in a town of 1,800: a glazed-tile Conoco station and café crowned with a neon-ringed tulip spire, built from a design its architect reportedly scratched into the dirt with a nail. Pixar drew it, nearly line-for-line, as Ramone's body shop in Cars.
see: The tower and the restored neon (lit at dusk — you're here midday, the tile is the show)
Inside: it's now Shamrock's visitor center and gift shop with the old café booths preserved; free, staffed by the friendliest retirees in Texas
There's a Tesla supercharger row behind it — the old road refueling the new one
tips: 20 minutes, right off I-40 exit 163
They'll stamp a Route 66 'passport' if you've picked one up anywhere; kids-menu-level joy, fully allowed for adults
d21s0.fact: When it opened, the U-Drop was 'the swankiest of swank eating places' per the local paper, with the nearest neon competition 160 km away. Its 2003 restoration cost 40× the original build price, and the town threw a parade.

d21s1.what: On April 19, 1995, a truck bomb destroyed the Murrah Federal Building and killed 168 people, including 19 children — the deadliest domestic terror attack in US history. The memorial that replaced it is one of the most effective in the world: 168 empty bronze chairs on the footprint, small chairs for the children, framed by twin gates stamped 9:01 and 9:03 — the minutes before and after.
see: The Field of Empty Chairs at the reflecting pool — each lit from within at night; the 19 small chairs stop everyone
The Survivor Tree: an American elm that took the blast at 40 m and lived; its saplings are planted at Sandy Hook, at the Boston Marathon finish, everywhere grief needs a tree
The Fence: a section of the original chain-link where people still leave tokens, 30 years on
tips: The outdoor memorial is free and open 24/7; ~45 min is right, and quiet is the local custom
The museum ($) is excellent but heavy — with your schedule, the grounds alone are the visit
It's a 5-min walk from Bricktown — do this first, dinner after
d21s1.fact: The Survivor Tree was nearly cut down for evidence — bomb fragments were embedded in its trunk. Arborists fought for it, and its annual seed harvest is now distributed to the public; the memorial's design contract legally requires its protection forever.

d21s2.what: Oklahoma City's old warehouse district, dead by 1970, was reborn around a canal nobody needed and everybody loves — water taxis loop past patios below street level. Tonight's actual mission is Oklahoma's great gift to cuisine: the onion burger, born in nearby El Reno in the 1920s when Depression cooks smashed cheap onions into expensive beef to stretch it.
see: The canal by water taxi or the free 20-min stroll of it
The Centennial Land Run Monument (east end): 45 larger-than-life bronze homesteaders mid-gallop — the 1889 land rush frozen at full chaos; weirdly great after dark
Flaming Lips Alley — the city named a street for its weirdest band
tips: Onion burger targets: Tucker's Onion Burgers (temple of the form) or S&B's for the creative versions
Bricktown parking garages are cheap; the district is compact and walkable
This is an easy night — you've earned a slow one; tomorrow is short
d21s2.fact: The fried-onion burger was engineering: onions were a nickel a sack, beef wasn't. El Reno still holds a Fried Onion Burger Day festival, and purists insist the onions must cook IN the smash, not beside it.

d22s0.what: In 1972, Hugh Davis — a retired zoo director — secretly built an 24-metre smiling sperm whale in his pond as a 34th-anniversary gift for his wife Zelda, who collected whale figurines. Passing Route 66 traffic assumed it was an attraction, so the family added picnic tables and let everyone in. It remains the mother road's purest artifact: a giant thing built for love.
see: The whale itself — walk into the smile, climb to the tail platforms; the blue concrete grin has been re-painted by volunteers for 50 years
The gift kiosk when staffed — proceeds keep Blue repainted; the caretakers know every inch of the story
tips: 20 minutes, free (donations welcome), picnic tables if you grabbed road snacks
5 min off I-44 at Catoosa — the detour is nothing, the photo is forever
d22s0.fact: Kids swam from the whale's dive platforms into the pond through the 1980s until insurance ended it. Zelda outlived Hugh and kept the whale; when paint faded in the 2000s, donations arrived from Route 66 clubs in Germany, Japan and Brazil.

d22s1.what: Branson is Nashville's unhinged country cousin — a town of 12,000 with 100+ live theaters — and the Landing is its lakefront: 2.4 km of boardwalk on Lake Taneycomo, a reservoir so deep and cold it holds trophy trout in the Ozark summer.
see: The fire-and-water fountain show at the center plaza — geysers, flames and music on the hour (evenings); unapologetically Vegas-lite and the kids next to you will lose their minds
The lake itself: Taneycomo's water comes from the bottom of an upstream dam at a constant 9°C — fog rolls off it on warm evenings like a horror film, beautifully
tips: Dinner on any boardwalk patio; the White River Fish House is the local pick
Early night, seriously — tomorrow is the 215-mile run to Six Flags St. Louis, and the 7:45 departure buys you a near-empty Boss at opening
If you ever return with more days: the $10 'Baldknobbers' or Presleys' shows are the authentic Branson experience, sequins and all
d22s1.fact: Branson's show industry started in 1959 with the Baldknobbers Jamboree — four brothers in a converted skating rink. The town now sells more theater seats per year than Broadway.

d23s0.what: Six Flags St. Louis (opened 1971 as Six Flags Over Mid-America, the last park the company built from scratch) sprawls across a Missouri hillside in Eureka — directly on your I-44 route into St. Louis, which is why it won the swap. It's a coaster park, not a theme park: three excellent wooden coasters in the trees, a launched Mr. Freeze, and a full waterpark (Hurricane Harbor) folded into the same ticket.
see: The Boss FIRST at opening: a terrain wooden monster at the very back of the park — 46 m drop into a ravine, famously rough-and-ferocious; lines are 5 min early, 60+ by 1pm
Screamin' Eagle (1976): when it opened it was the tallest, fastest wooden coaster on Earth — an out-and-back airtime machine through the woods
American Thunder: the modern GCI woodie — smooth, relentless, arguably the park's best ride; back row at dusk
Mr. Freeze: Reverse Blast — 0–110 km/h in 4 seconds, straight up a 66 m spike, then the whole thing backwards
tips: Weekday July hours run ~11am–8/9pm — your 7:45 Branson departure lands you at the gate as it opens; check the Six Flags app for today's exact close
Buy tickets online before Jul 27 (~$35–45 vs ~$85 at the gate); parking ~$35, I-44 exit 261
Hurricane Harbor is included — do coasters 11–2, waterpark through the 2–5pm peak heat (swim stuff + towel in the day bag), then back out for evening rides
Lockers at the waterpark; the park is hilly — you are once again walking up and down a Missouri hollow all day
d23s0.fact: The park's Screamin' Eagle was designer John Allen's last great wooden coaster — he retired believing wooden coasters had peaked with it. When it opened in 1976 it broke the world wood records for height, length AND speed, held for two years.

d23s1.what: Ted Drewes has sold frozen custard on old Route 66 since the Depression (the family business dates to 1929, this Chippewa Ave stand to 1941), and the 'concrete' — custard so dense it's handed to you upside down through the window — was invented here. St. Louis custard: eggs in the mix, colder machines, no air. It's better than ice cream and locals will fight you.
see: The upside-down hand-off — every concrete is flipped at the window; it doesn't fall, it never falls
The queue itself: a dozen windows, half of St. Louis, church clothes to softball uniforms — it moves in minutes and IS the experience
tips: Order: a 'Fox Treat' (hot fudge, raspberries, macadamia) or chocolate-chip-banana concrete, size small (their small is not small)
You're arriving after a full theme-park day — custard IS dinner, nobody is judging; open till ~11pm
Cash and card both fine; eat on the tailgate like everyone else
d23s1.fact: In winter the stand sells Christmas trees — 90 years and the family never bothered to open a second product line beyond custard and conifers. Ted Drewes Jr. ran it into his 90s.

d23s2.what: The Gateway Arch is the tallest monument in the US — 192 m of stainless steel catenary, Eero Saarinen's 1947 competition design finally topped out in 1965. You've done downtown St. Louis before and the tram pods weren't the draw — so tonight is the free version: the riverfront grounds at dusk, which is when the steel does its best trick anyway.
see: The arch from directly underneath — stand between the legs and look straight up the curve; it's free, it's vertiginous, and it's the angle the tram crowd forgets
Dusk light on the steel: the legs catch the last sun and glow rose-gold against the sky — better than any daytime view
The full-curve photo from the Old Courthouse steps (west side) — the whole 192 m fits in frame
tips: Grounds are free and open late; parking garages around the stadium are the easy option after ~7pm
Skipping the tram saves the timed-slot hassle — if you ever want it, that's a next-trip thing (you said it yourself)
45 minutes is right; Ted Drewes on the way in was dinner, this is the digestif
d23s2.fact: Each leg was built independently to meet at the top — a 0.4 mm error at the base would have missed. On closing day the south leg had expanded in the sun; fire hoses cooled it until the final piece slid in. The engineers' margin note: 'it fit.'

d24s0.what: Pontiac, Illinois is the mother road's museum town: the Route 66 Hall of Fame fills an old fire station with donated artifacts from every diner, gas station and motel the interstate killed, and 20+ murals cover the brick downtown. It's the last themed stop of the trip — the road home saying goodbye properly.
see: Bob Waldmire's VW microbus and school-bus 'road yacht' — the itinerant Route 66 artist lived on the road for decades; Pixar based Fillmore (the hippie van in Cars) on him and his bus
The giant Route 66 shield mural on the museum's back wall — the trip-closing photo op, truck in frame
The walldogs murals downtown — 20 minutes of strolling gets them all
tips: Free, donations welcomed, ~1 h with the murals; closed by late afternoon — your 9am departure has you here perfectly by late morning
The swinging pedestrian bridges over the Vermilion River (2 blocks east) are a genuine oddity if legs want a stretch
d24s0.fact: Waldmire, a vegetarian, politely declined Pixar's request to name the character after him because Fillmore would be sold as a McDonald's Happy Meal toy. They named it Fillmore; the bus is still his.

d24s1.what: Deep-dish is Chicago's parting argument: pizza as casserole — butter crust pressed up the pan walls, mozzarella DOWN first, toppings, then crushed tomatoes on top, because 45 minutes of baking would incinerate cheese on top. Lou Malnati learned the form at the original Pizzeria Uno in the 1940s and his family perfected it from 1971.
see: Order the Malnati Chicago Classic: buttercrust, their famous lean sausage as a single full-coverage patty (not crumbles — a disc), and the bright tomato top
The first cross-section lift — structural engineering in food form; phone cameras exist for this
tips: CALL AHEAD or order online when you leave Pontiac: deep dish takes 45 min to bake and they'll time it to your arrival
One 'medium' feeds two travelers who had custard for lunch yesterday; the 'personal' is a lie for one hungry person
Tap Lou's app/site for the frozen ship-home option — Tastes of Chicago ships anywhere in the US if anyone stateside deserves a gift
d24s1.fact: The deep-dish origin (Pizzeria Uno, 1943) is contested between Ike Sewell's steak-money and bartender Rudy Malnati's recipe — Lou was Rudy's son, so tonight you're eating the family's side of the argument. Chicagoans, privately, eat thin 'tavern-cut' more often; tourists get the monument.

d25s0.what: The unglamorous final boss: return the pickup full, clean-ish and on time at O'Hare's consolidated rental facility, then ride the ATS train to the terminals. Build slack into every step and the day stays boring, which is the goal.
see: One last odometer photo — you'll want the final number: ~5,200 miles
tips: Fuel within 15 km of the airport and KEEP THE RECEIPT — agencies dispute 'full' tanks; a timestamped receipt ends the conversation
Photograph/video the truck from all sides + interior at the return lane, with the agent's paperwork if possible
Empty the cooler, check under seats, door pockets, and the bed toolbox — 24 nights breed forgotten sunglasses
Allow 30–45 min at the return + 10 min ATS train to terminals
d25s0.fact: Your odometer total will read roughly the distance from Chicago to Istanbul — you drove to Turkey, through five climate zones, without leaving one country.

d25s1.what: O'Hare, by 13:30, boarding passes on phones, cooler-tanned and 5,000 miles wiser. Güle güle, America.
see: Terminal 5 (international) has decent food post-security; eat before the flight, airplane dinner is at weird body-clock time anyway
tips: International = 3 h buffer, which is exactly what the itinerary gave you; if TSA is kind, that's a long lunch, not wasted time
Download/offload photos to two places before boarding — the trip archive deserves redundancy
Open the app one last time at the gate and hit the Map tab: the loop is closed
d25s1.fact: O'Hare's code ORD comes from its old name, Orchard Field. The airport handles a takeoff or landing roughly every 30 seconds — one of them, today, closes a 25-day, 15-state loop of a lifetime.
